# Substrukt — Agent Instructions

Substrukt is a schema-driven CMS built in Rust. Content types are defined as JSON
Schemas. Each schema produces a set of JSON entries stored on disk. All content is
readable and writable via a REST API authenticated with a bearer token.

This file tells you everything you need to operate Substrukt on behalf of a user.
Read it fully before making any API calls.

---

## Authentication

Every API request must include this header:

```
Authorization: Bearer <token>
```

The token is provided by the user. There are three roles:

- `viewer` — read-only access to content and schemas
- `editor` — can create, update, and delete content entries
- `admin`  — editor permissions plus export and import

If a request returns 403, the token does not have sufficient role for that operation.

---

## Base URL

All API endpoints are relative to the deployment address the user provides.
Example: if the user says their site is at `https://mycms.example.com`, then the
schemas endpoint is `https://mycms.example.com/api/v1/schemas`.

---

## Data model

A **schema** defines a content type. Each schema has:
- a `slug` (URL-safe identifier, e.g. `blog-posts`)
- a `title` (human-readable name, e.g. `Blog Posts`)
- a `kind`: either `collection` (many entries) or `single` (exactly one entry)
- a `storage` mode: either `directory` (one file per entry) or `single-file`
- a `schema` object: standard JSON Schema describing the fields

A **content entry** is a JSON object that conforms to a schema's fields.
Entries in a `collection` schema each have a unique `id` (auto-generated from the
first string field, slugified). Entries in a `single` schema have no id.

No fields are enforced as required by default. The `required` array in a schema is
empty unless a user has explicitly edited it. Do not add fields to `required` when
creating or editing schemas.

---

## Endpoints

### Schemas

#### List all schemas
```
GET /api/v1/schemas
```
Returns an array. Each item has: `title`, `slug`, `storage`, `kind`, `schema`.
Call this first to discover what content types exist on this instance.

Response example:
```json
[
  {
    "title": "Blog Posts",
    "slug": "blog-posts",
    "storage": "directory",
    "kind": "collection",
    "schema": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "title": { "type": "string" },
        "body":  { "type": "string", "format": "textarea" },
        "published_at": { "type": "string" }
      },
      "required": []
    }
  }
]
```

#### Get a single schema
```
GET /api/v1/schemas/:slug
```
Returns the raw JSON Schema object for that slug.

---

### Content — collection kind

Use these endpoints for schemas where `kind` is `collection`.

#### List entries
```
GET /api/v1/content/:schema_slug
```
Query parameters:
- `status` — filter by status. Values: `published` (default), `draft`, `all`.
  Pass `?status=all` to retrieve every entry including drafts.
- `q` — keyword filter. Searches across all string field values.

Returns a JSON array of entry objects. Each entry contains the fields defined in
the schema. There is no `id` field in the response body; the id is only returned
on creation (see below).

#### Get a single entry
```
GET /api/v1/content/:schema_slug/:entry_id
```
Returns a single entry object, or 404 if not found.

#### Create an entry
```
POST /api/v1/content/:schema_slug
Content-Type: application/json
```
Body: a JSON object with the fields you want to set. Only `editor` role or above
can call this.

On success returns `201` with body `{"id": "<generated-id>"}`.
The id is derived from the first string field value, slugified. For example, a
post with `"title": "Hello World"` gets id `hello-world`.

Example body:
```json
{
  "title": "Hello World",
  "body": "This is my first post.",
  "published_at": "2024-01-15"
}
```

#### Update an entry
```
PUT /api/v1/content/:schema_slug/:entry_id
Content-Type: application/json
```
Body: a complete JSON object with all fields (full replacement, not a patch).
Returns `200` on success.

#### Delete an entry
```
DELETE /api/v1/content/:schema_slug/:entry_id
```
Returns `204` on success.

---

### Content — single kind

Use these endpoints for schemas where `kind` is `single`. These schemas have
exactly one entry with no id.

#### Get the entry
```
GET /api/v1/content/:schema_slug/single
```
Returns the entry object, or 404 if not yet set.

#### Create or update the entry
```
PUT /api/v1/content/:schema_slug/single
Content-Type: application/json
```
Body: a JSON object with the fields to set. This upserts — it creates if absent,
updates if present. Returns `200` on success.

#### Delete the entry
```
DELETE /api/v1/content/:schema_slug/single
```
Returns `204` on success.

---

### Uploads

File uploads are stored content-addressed (SHA-256). The `upload` format field
type stores a reference object `{"hash": "...", "filename": "...", "mime": "..."}`.

#### Upload a file
```
POST /api/v1/uploads
Content-Type: multipart/form-data
```
Include the file as a multipart field. Returns:
```json
{
  "hash": "<sha256>",
  "filename": "image.png",
  "mime": "image/png",
  "size": 12345
}
```
To set an upload field on an entry, use this object as the field value.

#### Retrieve a file
```
GET /api/v1/uploads/:hash
```
Returns the raw file bytes with correct Content-Type header.

---

### Export and import

These require `admin` role.

#### Export a bundle
```
POST /api/v1/export
```
Returns a `tar.gz` file containing all schemas, content, and uploads.

#### Import a bundle
```
POST /api/v1/import
Content-Type: multipart/form-data
```
Include the bundle as a multipart field named `bundle`. This overwrites all
current content with the bundle contents. Returns `{"status": "ok"}`.

---

### Publish

Triggers a configured webhook to rebuild the static site.

```
POST /api/v1/publish/staging
POST /api/v1/publish/production
```
- `staging` fires the staging webhook immediately.
- `production` promotes all drafts to published, then fires the production webhook.

Returns `{"status": "triggered"}` on success, or 404 if no webhook is configured
for that environment.

---

## Workflows

These are the most common multi-step tasks. Follow each sequence in order.

### Read existing content and add new entries matching the style

1. Call `GET /api/v1/schemas` to discover available content types and their fields.
2. Identify the relevant schema slug from the response (e.g. `blog-posts`).
3. Call `GET /api/v1/content/<slug>?status=all` to read all existing entries.
4. Analyse the existing entries to understand tone, structure, and field usage.
5. For each new entry, construct a JSON body using the same fields and style.
6. Call `POST /api/v1/content/<slug>` for each new entry.
7. Optionally call `POST /api/v1/publish/staging` to trigger a rebuild.

### Update site settings or global configuration

1. Call `GET /api/v1/schemas` and find schemas with `kind: "single"`.
2. Call `GET /api/v1/content/<slug>/single` to read the current value.
3. Modify the fields the user requested.
4. Call `PUT /api/v1/content/<slug>/single` with the full updated object.

### Edit an existing entry

1. Call `GET /api/v1/content/<slug>?status=all&q=<keyword>` to locate the entry.
2. Note the entry's identifying field value to reconstruct its id (slugified first
   string field). Or call `GET /api/v1/content/<slug>` and find by field value.
3. Call `GET /api/v1/content/<slug>/<id>` to retrieve the full entry.
4. Apply the user's requested changes to the entry object.
5. Call `PUT /api/v1/content/<slug>/<id>` with the full modified object.

### Create a new content type (schema)

Schemas are managed through the CMS web UI, not the API. If a user asks you to
create a new content type, instruct them to go to the Schemas section in the CMS
UI and create it there. Once created, the new schema and its slug will appear in
`GET /api/v1/schemas`.

---

## Rules and constraints

- Never add fields to the `required` array in a schema. Leave `required` as `[]`.
- When updating an entry, always send the complete object — partial patches are
  not supported. First read the entry, then modify, then write the full object back.
- Entry ids are auto-generated on creation from the first string field. You cannot
  choose the id manually.
- The `?status=all` parameter is required to see draft entries. The default is
  `published` only.
- The `single` kind uses a different URL pattern than `collection`. Do not use
  `/content/:slug/:id` for single-kind schemas; use `/content/:slug/single`.
- Rate limit is 100 API requests per minute per IP. Space out bulk operations if
  creating many entries at once.
